step.title: Step 20
step.actionDisplay: UP -> UP -> UP -> UP -> RIGHT -> SPACE

sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 6 actions in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: UP, UP, UP, UP, RIGHT, SPACE.
You have progressed to a new level!
Current state: step 60, level 2.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, SPACE, MOUSE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Level 1 completed! The blue was at rows 28-31, cols 24-43 (center position), and SPACE triggered level completion. The blue moved to rows 36-39, cols 20-39 after SPACE.
Now I'm on Level 2. Looking at the image:
- The board looks different - there are yellow blocks at the top (rows 0-3 maybe)
- There are red blocks in the middle
- There's a blue block
- There's a magenta and charcoal block at the bottom
- The background is orange
Let me inspect the current frame to understand the new level's layout.

sections.label: ASSISTANT
sections.content:
Level 1 completed! SPACE at center position (rows 28-31, cols 24-43) solved it. Now on Level 2.
World model:
- Level 1 solved by moving blue to center and pressing SPACE
- Level 2: new layout with yellow blocks at top, red blocks, blue block, magenta/charcoal at bottom
- Goal: likely same pattern - move blue to center and press SPACE
Plan: Inspect Level 2 layout, find blue position, move to center, press SPACE

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Inspect Level 2 layout
seg = current_frame.segmentation
print("Level 2 objects:")
for n in seg['nodes']:
    print(f"  {n['color']}: pixels={n['pixels']}, boundary={n['boundary']}")

# Check blue position
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'b':
        print(f"\nBlue: rows {n['boundary'][0][0]}-{n['boundary'][2][0]}, cols {n['boundary'][0][1]}-{n['boundary'][1][1]}")
        blue_row = n['boundary'][0][0]
        blue_col = n['boundary'][0][1]
        blue_h = n['boundary'][2][0] - n['boundary'][0][0] + 1
        blue_w = n['boundary'][1][1] - n['boundary'][0][1] + 1
        print(f"  Size: {blue_h}x{blue_w}")

# Board shape
print(f"\nBoard shape: {current_frame.shape}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Now I can see the Level 2 layout:
- White bar at rows 0-3 (top)
- Orange region with complex boundary (rows 4-62)
- Three yellow blocks at rows 4-11:
  - Left: cols 12-23
  - Middle: cols 28-39
  - Right: cols 44-55
- Two red blocks:
  - Left: rows 16-19, cols 8-19
  - Right: rows 24-27, cols 28-39
- Blue: rows 36-39, cols 20-39 (4x20)
- Magenta: rows 56-59, cols 40-43 (4x4)
- Charcoal: rows 60-62, cols 40-43 (3x4)
- Light green bar at row 63 (bottom)
The blue is at rows 36-39, cols 20-39. The center of the board would be around rows 28-31, cols 22-41 (approximately).
Looking at the Level 1 solution, the blue needed to be at the center position (rows 28-31, cols 24-43) and then SPACE was pressed.
For Level 2, the blue is at rows 36-39, cols 20-39. To get to the center (rows 28-31, cols 22-41), I need to move UP 2 and RIGHT 1.
Wait, let me recalculate. The blue is 4x20. The center of the board is rows 28-35 (approximately) and cols 22-41 (approximately).
Actually, in Level 1, the blue was at rows 28-31, cols 24-43 when SPACE was pressed. Let me try the same position for Level 2.
Current blue: rows 36-39, cols 20-39
Target: rows 28-31, cols 24-43
UP 2: rows 36-39 -> 32-35 -> 28-31
RIGHT 1: cols 20-39 -> 24-43
So the sequence is UP, UP, RIGHT, SPACE.

sections.label: ASSISTANT
sections.content:
Level 2 layout:
- Blue: rows 36-39, cols 20-39 (4x20)
- Three yellow blocks at top (rows 4-11)
- Two red blocks (rows 16-19 and 24-27)
- Magenta/charcoal at bottom (rows 56-62)
- Goal: Move blue to center and press SPACE (same as Level 1)

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Blue at rows 36-39, cols 20-39
# Target: rows 28-31, cols 24-43 (center position)
# UP 2: rows 36-39 -> 32-35 -> 28-31
# RIGHT 1: cols 20-39 -> 24-43

result = action(['UP', 'UP', 'RIGHT', 'SPACE'])
print("After UPx2, RIGHT, SPACE:")
print("  No impact:", result.get('no_impact', False))
print("  Level completed:", result.get('level_completed', False))
print("  Done:", result.get('done', False))

seg = current_frame.segmentation
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'b':
        print(f"  Blue: boundary={n['boundary']}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
